It's extremely important that we only employ the principles of persuasion in ethical ways that are mutually beneficial. Ethics are so important to the future of our relationships with customers or coworkers that we need to pay close attention to the rewards and costs of influencing in ethically commendable versus ethically objectionable ways. Let's dive into some of the concepts that define the ethics of persuasion. If we look globally at how ethical influence is practiced to enhance relationships, there are three criteria we should use to characterize an influence appeal. Is it true? Is it natural? And is it wise? First, is it true? Is our claim honest, accurate, and factual? This doesn't mean that we have to tell somebody absolutely everything we know, we rarely have time for that. But we do need to describe whatever is materially relevant to making a well-informed decision. Second, is it naturally there? Is the principle we are using inherently present in the situation, that is, not exaggerated or counterfeited or imported artificially into the influence setting in any way? An example of what we mean by naturally present can help here. There was an avid water skier who saw only one pair of skis left at his favorite store and asked the salesperson if it was the last set. The salesperson said, yes it is. So the customer bought the skis. But on a visit to the store a few days later, he saw a full rack of the same skis. Confused, he hailed down the salesperson and said, hey, I thought this type of skis I bought last week were the last ones. To which the salesperson replied, well, they were the last ones in the store. We had more back in the warehouse, but you didn't ask me about the warehouse. The customer, as you can imagine, felt entirely misled about the naturally existing scarcity of that type of skis. You can probably also guess the result. He never returned to that store. Finally, there's the third component that defines an ethical influence attempt. Is it wise? Would it be wise for both parties if the recipient says yes to the communicator's request? Although there are costs and benefits for both sides in any exchange, is the overall net benefit favorable for both individuals?
